I started a separate trading portfolio in the X subscription before the year started that is focused entirely on options (capped at 10 names) where it's built for shorter-term setups where I can clearly see the catalyst ahead and use options to keep the risk defined.

MY 3 BIGGEST TRADING PORTFOLIO WINNERS THIS YEAR

1. $AMKR $50 calls expiring Jan 28 (+529% gain)
Amkor is the advanced packaging layer that helps solve the CPU bottleneck through chiplets, heterogeneous integration  high-density interconnects as CPU + GPU architectures become more tightly integrated.

2. $VG $7.50 calls expiring Jan 28 (+368% gain)
Venture Global was the cleanest way for me to capitalize on the Iran war because LNG had shifted into an energy security trade where every LNG-dependent country now understands the value of diversified contracts outside a hot zone.

3. $NVTS $10 calls expiring Jan 28 (+181% gain)
Navitas is the power conversion layer, where GaN becomes increasingly relevant as next-gen 800V data center architectures demand more efficiency, less heat and higher power density.

$NOW CEO Bill McDermott says the market is pricing in fear that LLMs could replace large parts of enterprise software.

But in an AI economy where companies need to operationalize agentic AI safely, ServiceNow looks more like the control layer for governance, context, routing and oversight.

The market is still struggling to separate software that gets disrupted by AI from software that becomes more valuable because AI needs exactly that control layer.

I think ServiceNow sits much closer to the second bucket.
$NVTS should say $12**

Fibonacci levels help you see where momentum pauses by mapping potential areas of support & resistance using ratios from the Fib sequence.  

I find them useful because strong trends often consolidate at these levels before continuing higher & this list reflects names consolidating between the 0.382  & 0.618 extension levels.

I favor the PEG ratio because it ties valuation to growth and I prefer using a 2026 to 2028 window so the multiple adjusts for how fast the business is actually compounding.

For example, $AMD may trade at ~52x forward earnings but earnings are expected to grow from $4.51 in 2026 to $12.62 in 2028 implying ~67% earnings CAGR so their PEG is ~0.8x.

PETER LYNCH‚ÄôS FAVORITE METRIC IS THE PEG RATIO

PEG < 1 usually means mispriced growth
PEG > 2 starts to push into the danger zone

Intel Q1 2026 Earnings Snapshot

- Intel‚Äôs stock surges 20% in after-hours trading, reaching an all-time high of $83.72 on April 23, 2026, after 26 years.

- The company reported Q1 2026 revenue of $12.67B, up by 7.2% YoY, marking a return to growth.

- Data Center & AI revenue hit $5.1B, growing 22% YoY, making it the primary growth driver.

- Non-GAAP EPS was $0.29, while GAAP EPS was -$0.73, reflecting ongoing restructuring costs.

-Intel reported a net loss of $4.28 billion, from $887 million a year earlier, highlighting that profitability remains a challenge despite revenue growth.

-Foundry revenue reached $5.4B (+16% YoY), though still largely driven by internal demand.

-Strong AI demand, particularly CPU demand for inference workloads, is driving growth, signalling a shift beyond GPU dominance led by Nvidia.

-Intel guided Q2 revenue to $13.8B-$14.8B, indicating continued momentum into the next quarter.

Source: Intel Investor Relations, CNBC
